content: I'll start by reading the required instruction files and then examine the Lean file and blueprint chapter.

1	---
2	name: lean-vs-blueprint-checker
3	description: Per-file bidirectional verifier. Reads one .lean file and its blueprint chapter; reports (a) whether the Lean file follows the blueprint — fake/placeholder statements, signature mismatches with \lean{...} hints, proof divergence, "temporary" defs — AND (b) whether the blueprint chapter is detailed enough to have guided a faithful formalization of this file.
4	write_domain: "task_results/**"
5	read_only: true
6	can_spawn: false
7	default_enabled: false
8	mandatory: [review]
9	dispatcher_notes: |
10	  - I am highly recommended in the review phase. Dispatch one checker
11	    per `.lean` file that received prover work this iteration. Each
12	    dispatch is independent and parallel-able.
13	
14	    **You may skip me this iter when NO `.lean` file received prover
15	    work** (e.g. the prover phase was skipped, or every prover
16	    INCOMPLETE'd without committing edits). Record under `## Subagent
17	    skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`:
18	    ``- lean-vs-blueprint-checker: no .lean files modified this iter
19	    (no prover edits to verify)``.
20	
21	    Do NOT skip a per-file dispatch when the prover DID commit edits
22	    to that file — skipping a prover-touched file is the review-phase
23	    failure this affordance exists to surface, not to enable. The
24	    "highly recommended" framing applies to the whole-iter decision,
25	    not to picking-and-choosing within an iter that has multiple
26	    prover-touched files.
27	  - Also dispatch on any file a code-audit subagent in your catalog
28	    flagged as suspicious, even if it received no prover work this
29	    iter.
30	  - The directive must name exactly one Lean file and its blueprint
31	    chapter — no global context, no strategy snapshot. My value is
32	    the narrow file-vs-chapter view.
33	  - I report bidirectionally: Lean → blueprint AND blueprint → Lean.
34	    The blueprint can be the failure (too thin to guide
35	    formalization, missing the level of detail the Lean code clearly
36	    needed), not only the Lean. If I flag a chapter as inadequate,
37	    the plan agent should dispatch the catalog's blueprint-writing
38	    subagent to address it.
39	  - Any must-fix-this-iter finding I report blocks downstream work on
40	    the affected file/chapter until addressed. Soft enforcement: the
41	    plan agent reads my report and follows the gate rules in plan.md.
42	---
43	
44	# Lean ↔ Blueprint Checker
45	
46	You compare **one Lean file** against **its blueprint chapter** and report whether the Lean follows the blueprint faithfully. You are read-only.
47	
48	## Your Job
49	
50	Your directive names one `.lean` file (e.g. `Algebra/WLocal.lean`) and its corresponding blueprint chapter (e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Algebra_WLocal.tex`). You audit **bidirectionally**:
51	
52	### (A) Does the Lean follow the blueprint?
53	
54	For each `\lean{...}`-tagged block in the chapter, verify the Lean side:
55	
56	- **Declaration exists** with the right name (matches `\lean{namespace.theorem_name}`).
57	- **Signature matches** the informal statement in the chapter — the types are what the prose says they should be. A statement that type-checks but uses the wrong type class (e.g. `Smooth f` where the prose says "smooth of relative dimension n") is **wrong**, not "approximate".
58	- **Proof follows the blueprint sketch** — the proof in the Lean file should be a formalization of the steps in the chapter's `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}`, not a different argument that happens to produce the same conclusion.
59	- **No fake / placeholder statements** — definitions or theorems whose body is just `:= sorry` or a trivially-true tautology while the chapter prose claims something substantive.
60	- **No "temporary" definitions left in place** — comments like `-- TODO: replace with the real definition`, `-- placeholder until we figure out X`, or definitions whose body is suspicious (e.g. `def Foo := True`, weakened-wrong definitions standing in for the real concept).
61	- **No comments that explain away wrong code** — "we are using a wrong definition for now", "this proof is incomplete but will be fixed", etc. These are red flags, not acceptable workflow notes.
62	
63	### (B) Is the blueprint adequate for this Lean file?
64	
65	The blueprint can also be the failure mode. For the chapter you're reading, check:
66	
67	- **Every Lean declaration in this file should map to a blueprint block** (via `\lean{...}`) OR be an explicitly-helper-only declaration. If the file has substantive declarations the blueprint doesn't reference at all, the chapter is incomplete.
68	- **Proof sketches in the blueprint should be detailed enough** for a prover to formalize correctly. If you see Lean proofs that clearly went through significant reasoning the blueprint's `\begin{proof}` does NOT preview, flag the chapter as under-specified.
69	- **`\lean{...}` hints should pin the Lean target precisely.** If the blueprint says "smooth morphism" without specifying which Mathlib predicate (`Smooth` vs `SmoothOfRelativeDimension n` vs `IsSmooth`), and the Lean file ended up with the wrong one, that's BOTH a Lean error AND a blueprint clarity failure. Flag both sides.
70	- **Definitions in the blueprint should be at the right level of generality** for the Lean to consume. If the blueprint defines something narrower than the project actually needs and the Lean had to write a parallel API to cover the gap, that's a blueprint adequacy failure.
71	
72	Both directions matter. Don't excuse a wrong Lean statement by saying "the blueprint is loose"; don't excuse a thin blueprint by saying "the Lean works anyway." Report both.
73	
74	## Directive Format
75	
76	```markdown
77	# Lean ↔ Blueprint Checker Directive
78	
79	## Slug
80	<slug>
81	
82	## Lean file
83	<path/to/file.lean>
84	
85	## Blueprint chapter
86	blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex
87	
88	## Known issues
89	<things the review agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
90	```
91	
92	That's it. No strategy snapshot, no references, no full project context — just the two files and any pre-known issues to skip. Cross-file consistency is the job of the code-audit and blueprint-review subagents in the catalog; you stay focused on this one pair.
93	
94	## What you do
95	
96	1. Read your directive.
97	2. Read the Lean file completely. Note every declaration (`def`, `theorem`, `lemma`, `instance`, `axiom`, …) and its signature.
98	3. Read the blueprint chapter completely. List every `\lean{...}` reference and its corresponding declaration block.
99	4. **For each `\lean{...}` block**: locate the Lean declaration. Verify:
100	   - It exists.
101	   - Its signature matches the informal statement (use `archon-lean-lsp` for hover/signature info).
102	   - If the Lean has a body (proof or definition body), does it match the blueprint's prose? Naming and tactic choice can differ; the *mathematical content* should match.
103	5. **For each declaration in the Lean file**: is it `\lean{...}`-referenced from the blueprint? Unreferenced declarations are notable but not necessarily wrong (helpers may exist). Flag suspect ones.
104	6. **Scan for red flags** in the Lean file:
105	   - `:= sorry` where the blueprint claims a substantive statement (placeholder).
106	   - Suspect bodies like `:= True`, `:= rfl` for non-trivial claims, `:= Classical.choice _`.
107	   - Comments excusing wrong/incomplete code: `-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- temporary`, `-- will fix later`, `-- this is wrong but works for now`.
108	   - `axiom` declarations.
109	7. **Cross-check** any `import` paths and namespace usage that should match what the blueprint's `\lean{...}` hints suggest.
110	
111	You may use:
112	- `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools (read-only): hover, signature lookup, diagnostics.
113	- `sorry_analyzer` if available, to count sorries in the file.
114	
115	You may NOT modify the Lean file, the blueprint, or any other project file. Even when a fix is obvious.
116	
117	## Report format
118	
119	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/lean-vs-blueprint-checker-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
120	
121	```markdown

133	
134	## Per-declaration

136	For every `\lean{...}` block in the chapter, one entry:
137	
138	### `\lean{Foo.bar}` (chapter: \thm:bar)
139	- **Lean target exists**: yes / no (if no: declaration name in the .lean that comes closest, or "none found")
140	- **Signature matches**: yes / partial / no (with one line on the mismatch)
141	- **Proof follows sketch**: yes / partial / no / N/A (if N/A: chapter doesn't have a proof body to compare against)
142	- **notes**: <free-form, one line per observation>
143	
144	### `\lean{Foo.baz}` ...
145	
146	## Red flags
147	
148	(Section appears only if there are findings.)
149	
150	### Placeholder / suspect bodies
151	- `Foo.bar` at line 42: body is `:= sorry` but blueprint claims a substantive proof.
152	- `Foo.qux` at line 78: defined as `:= True`, suspect.
153	- ...
154	
155	### Excuse-comments
156	- `Foo.lean:113`: comment "TODO: replace with real def" attached to `Foo.frob`. Blueprint claims `Foo.frob` is the actual definition, not a placeholder.
157	- ...
158	
159	### Axioms / Classical.choice on non-trivial claims
160	- `Foo.lean:200`: `axiom` declaration `Foo.skip_step` introduced. Blueprint does not authorize this.
161	
162	## Unreferenced declarations (informational)
163	
164	<list of declarations in the Lean file that no `\lean{...}` references. Most are helpers — only flag the ones whose name suggests they should be in the blueprint.>
165	
166	## Blueprint adequacy for this file
167	
168	A bidirectional check: does the blueprint chapter give a prover enough detail to formalize this file correctly?
169	
170	- **Coverage**: <N>/<M> Lean declarations have a corresponding `\lean{...}` block in the chapter. Unreferenced declarations: <count> helpers (acceptable) + <count> substantive (flagged below).
171	- **Proof-sketch depth**: <one of: adequate / under-specified / silent>. If under-specified or silent, list which `\thm:...` blocks need expansion.
172	- **Hint precision**: <one of: precise / loose / wrong>. Loose hints (the prose pins one Mathlib predicate but the `\lean{...}` doesn't, leaving the prover to guess) get flagged. Wrong hints (the `\lean{...}` names a declaration whose signature doesn't match the prose) are critical.
173	- **Generality**: <one of: matches need / too narrow / too broad>. If too narrow, name the parallel API that the project ended up writing because the blueprint didn't cover the needed level of generality.
174	- **Recommended chapter-side actions**: <bullet list of items a blueprint-writing subagent should land if you flagged anything above>
175	
176	## Severity summary
177	
178	Apply these rules verbatim. Do NOT under-classify to soften the blow — the plan agent's gates depend on accurate severity, and a hidden critical finding hardens into shipped wrong code.
179	
180	- **must-fix-this-iter** — every one of the following lands here, no exceptions:
181	  - Placeholder body (`:= sorry`, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial claim, suspect `:= Classical.choice _` patterns) on a declaration the blueprint claims is substantive.
182	  - Signature mismatch with the blueprint's prose — including using the wrong Mathlib predicate (e.g. `Smooth` vs `SmoothOfRelativeDimension n`) when the prose pins one specific predicate.
183	  - Excuse-comments (`-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- temporary`, `-- placeholder`, `-- wrong but works for now`) on declarations the blueprint claims are real.
184	  - Axioms or `Classical.choice _` on substantive claims the blueprint does NOT authorize.
185	  - Blueprint adequacy failure: chapter is so under-specified that a prover could not have formalized this file correctly from prose alone.
186	  - Weakened-wrong definitions: Lean defines a structurally-different stand-in (e.g. `Scheme.LineBundle := CommRing.Pic Γ(X,⊤)` when the blueprint defines it as an invertible quasi-coherent O_X-module).
187	- **major** — partial signature mismatches that are fixable in-place, missing `\lean{...}` references to declarations that the blueprint should reference, comments that are stale but not actively misleading.
188	- **minor** — naming drift, helpers worth promoting to blueprint blocks, low-impact prose-vs-Lean differences.
189	
190	Overall verdict: one sentence.
191	```
192	
193	The plan agent's per-file gate (see plan.md and the blueprint-review subagent's `dispatcher_notes` in the catalog) treats every must-fix-this-iter finding as blocking. If you classify a wrong signature as "major" to avoid the gate firing, you are working against the project, not for it.
194	
195	## Return value
196	
197	Your final assistant message:
198	
199	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> declarations checked, <M> red flags`
200	- The path to your full report.
201	
202	## Reminders
203	
204	- **One file pair, no global context.** Cross-file consistency belongs to the catalog's code-audit and blueprint-review subagents.
205	- **You are read-only.** No project source, no blueprint, no state files (except your own report).
206	- **Excuse-comments are red flags, not workflow.** Treat "we use a wrong def for now" with the suspicion it deserves.
207	- **Mathematical content match, not syntactic.** The Lean proof can choose different tactics than the chapter's sketch implies, as long as the mathematical steps are the same.
208

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

67	/-! ## §0. Presheaf-level: the dual of the monoidal unit is the unit
68	
69	Project-local supplement to `PresheafInternalHom.lean`: `PresheafOfModules.dual 𝟙_ ≅ 𝟙_`
70	(the evaluation-at-`1` isomorphism `ℋom(𝟙_, 𝟙_) ≅ 𝟙_`), built over a general single-universe
71	base category.  It feeds `Scheme.Modules.dual_unit_iso` (below) at `R₀ := Y.presheaf`. -/

74	
75	open InternalHom Opposite
76	
77	variable {D : Type u} [Category.{u, u} D] {R₀ : Dᵒᵖ ⥤ CommRingCat.{u}}
78	
79	/-- **Section equivalence for the dual of the unit.** At an object `X`, endomorphisms of the
80	(restricted) unit `restr X 𝟙_ ⟶ restr X 𝟙_` are identified `R₀(X)`-linearly with `R₀(X)` itself,
81	via evaluation at `1`; the inverse is multiplication by a global scalar (`globalSMul`). The
82	substantive content is `left_inv`: every endomorphism of the unit is multiplication by its value
83	at `1` (proved from `φ`-naturality toward the terminal object of the slice). -/
84	noncomputable def unitDualSectionEquiv (X : Dᵒᵖ) :
85	    letI := internalHomObjModule X.unop
86	      (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))
87	      (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))
88	    (restr X.unop (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))) ⟶
89	        restr X.unop (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))))
90	      ≃ₗ[(R₀.obj (op X.unop) : Type u)] (R₀.obj (op X.unop) : Type u) := by
91	  letI := internalHomObjModule X.unop
92	    (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))
93	    (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))
94	  exact
95	    { toFun := fun φ =>
96	        evalLin (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))) X φ
97	          (1 : ((R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat).obj X : Type u))
98	      map_add' := fun φ φ' => rfl
99	      map_smul' := fun c φ => by
100	        exact DFunLike.congr_fun (evalLin_smul _ X c φ)
101	          (1 : ((R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat).obj X : Type u))
102	      invFun := fun r =>
103	        globalSMul Over.mkIdTerminal
104	          (restr X.unop (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))) r
105	      left_inv := fun φ => by
106	        ext Y
107	        dsimp only
108	        erw [globalSMul_hom_apply]
109	        have hnat := PresheafOfModules.naturality_apply φ (Over.mkIdTerminal.from Y.unop).op
110	          (1 : ((R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat).obj X : Type u))
111	        erw [PresheafOfModules.unit_map_one] at hnat
112	        erw [hnat, smul_eq_mul, mul_one]
113	        rfl
114	      right_inv := fun r => by
115	        change ((globalSMul Over.mkIdTerminal
116	            (restr X.unop
117	              (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))) r).app
118	            (op (Over.mk (𝟙 X.unop)))).hom
119	            (1 : ((R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat).obj X : Type u)) = r
120	        rw [globalSMul_hom_apply, termRingMap_terminal]
121	        exact mul_one r }
122	
123	/-- **The presheaf dual of the monoidal unit is the unit**, `PresheafOfModules.dual 𝟙_ ≅ 𝟙_`,
124	assembled sectionwise from `unitDualSectionEquiv` with the evaluation-at-`1` naturality (mirroring
125	`InternalHom.internalHomEval`'s naturality at `M = 𝟙_`). -/
126	noncomputable def dualUnitIsoGen :
127	    PresheafOfModules.dual (R₀ := R₀)
128	        (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))
129	      ≅ 𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)) :=
130	  PresheafOfModules.isoMk (fun X => (unitDualSectionEquiv X).toModuleIso)
131	    (fun {X Y} f => by
132	      refine ModuleCat.hom_ext (LinearMap.ext fun φ => ?_)
133	      change evalLin (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))) Y
134	            ((PresheafOfModules.dual
135	              (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))).map f φ)
136	            (1 : ((R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat).obj Y : Type u))
137	          = ((𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))).map f).hom
138	              (evalLin (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))) X φ
139	                (1 : ((R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat).obj X : Type u)))
140	      have key := PresheafOfModules.naturality_apply
141	        (φ : restr X.unop (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))) ⟶
142	          restr X.unop (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))))
143	        (Over.homMk f.unop : Over.mk f.unop ⟶ Over.mk (𝟙 X.unop)).op
144	        (1 : ((R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat).obj X : Type u))
145	      have hrm : (restr X.unop
146	            (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))).map
147	          (Over.homMk f.unop : Over.mk f.unop ⟶ Over.mk (𝟙 X.unop)).op
148	          = (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))).map f := rfl
149	      rw [hrm] at key
150	      erw [PresheafOfModules.unit_map_one] at key
151	      have hAB : (op (Over.mk (𝟙 Y.unop ≫ f.unop)) : (Over X.unop)ᵒᵖ) = op (Over.mk f.unop) :=
152	        congrArg op (congrArg Over.mk (Category.id_comp f.unop))
153	      have homAppHEq : ∀ {A B : (Over X.unop)ᵒᵖ} (_ : A = B), HEq (φ.app A) (φ.app B) := by
154	        intro A B h; subst h; rfl
155	      have hdt : evalLin (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))) Y
156	          ((PresheafOfModules.dual
157	            (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))).map f φ)
158	          = (φ.app (op (Over.mk f.unop))).hom :=
159	        congrArg ModuleCat.Hom.hom (eq_of_heq (homAppHEq hAB))
160	      exact (DFunLike.congr_fun hdt _).trans key)
161	
162	end PresheafOfModules

170	/-! ## §A. The C-bridge: restriction commutes with the sheaf-level dual -/
171	
172	open Opposite in
173	/-- **Leg-B atomic claim: the lax-monoidal unit `ε` of `restrictScalars` along the open-immersion
174	structure ring iso `(f.appIso W').inv` is an isomorphism.**  Its underlying map is the (bijective)
175	ring map `(f.appIso W').inv.hom`, so `ε` is an iso by `restrictScalars_isIso_ε_of_bijective`
176	(`PresheafInternalHom.lean`) fed the bijectivity from `ConcreteCategory.bijective_of_isIso`.  This
177	is the single load-bearing fact powering `dualUnitRingSwap` (the codomain unit ring swap of leg-B),
178	phrased at the `CommRingCat` carrier so `CommRing` is native (per `analogies/ma-legb262.md`). -/
179	lemma isIso_ε_restrictScalars_appIso {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : Y ⟶ X) [IsOpenImmersion f]
180	    (W' : TopologicalSpace.Opens ↥Y) :
181	    IsIso (Functor.LaxMonoidal.ε
182	      (ModuleCat.restrictScalars (Scheme.Hom.appIso f W').inv.hom)) :=
183	  restrictScalars_isIso_ε_of_bijective (Scheme.Hom.appIso f W').inv.hom
184	    (CategoryTheory.ConcreteCategory.bijective_of_isIso (Scheme.Hom.appIso f W').inv)
185	
186	open Opposite in
187	/-- **Leg-B: the codomain unit ring-iso swap** `restrictScalars (f.appIso W').inv (𝟙_X(fW')) ⟶
188	𝟙_Y(W')`.  It is the inverse of the lax-monoidal unit `ε (restrictScalars (f.appIso W').inv.hom)`,
189	an isomorphism by `isIso_ε_restrictScalars_appIso`.  The endpoints are written at the canonical
190	`CommRingCat` section carriers `↑(X.presheaf.obj _)` / `↑(Y.presheaf.obj _)` (the `forget₂`-composite
191	carrier breaks `MonoidalCategoryStruct` synthesis, `analogies/ma-legb262.md`); they reconcile by
192	`rfl`/defeq with the `restr`/`𝟙_`-section spellings of `sliceDualTransport`'s `codomainMap` hole. -/
193	noncomputable def dualUnitRingSwap {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : Y ⟶ X) [IsOpenImmersion f]
194	    (W' : TopologicalSpace.Opens ↥Y) :
195	    (ModuleCat.restrictScalars (Scheme.Hom.appIso f W').inv.hom).obj
196	        (𝟙_ (ModuleCat ↑(X.presheaf.obj (op ((Scheme.Hom.opensFunctor f).obj W'))))) ⟶
197	      𝟙_ (ModuleCat ↑(Y.presheaf.obj (op W'))) :=
198	  haveI := isIso_ε_restrictScalars_appIso f W'
199	  CategoryTheory.inv (Functor.LaxMonoidal.ε
200	    (ModuleCat.restrictScalars (Scheme.Hom.appIso f W').inv.hom))
201	
202	open Opposite in
203	/-- **Leg-B (inverse direction): the unit codomain ring-iso swap for `invFun`** `𝟙_Y(W') ⟶
204	restrictScalars (f.appIso W').inv (𝟙_X(fW'))`.  This is the lax-monoidal unit
205	`ε (restrictScalars (f.appIso W').inv.hom)` ITSELF (not its inverse), the reverse of
206	`dualUnitRingSwap`.  By `isIso_ε_restrictScalars_appIso` it is an isomorphism and is the inverse of
207	`dualUnitRingSwap f W'` (they cancel by `IsIso.inv_hom_id`/`hom_inv_id`). -/
208	noncomputable def dualUnitRingSwapInv {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : Y ⟶ X) [IsOpenImmersion f]
209	    (W' : TopologicalSpace.Opens ↥Y) :
210	    (𝟙_ (ModuleCat ↑(Y.presheaf.obj (op W')))) ⟶
211	      (ModuleCat.restrictScalars (Scheme.Hom.appIso f W').inv.hom).obj
212	        (𝟙_ (ModuleCat ↑(X.presheaf.obj (op ((Scheme.Hom.opensFunctor f).obj W'))))) :=
213	  Functor.LaxMonoidal.ε (ModuleCat.restrictScalars (Scheme.Hom.appIso f W').inv.hom)
214	
215	open Opposite in
216	/-- `dualUnitRingSwapInv` is a section of `dualUnitRingSwap` (`ε ≫ inv ε = 𝟙`). -/
217	@[simp] lemma dualUnitRingSwapInv_comp_dualUnitRingSwap {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : Y ⟶ X)
218	    [IsOpenImmersion f] (W' : TopologicalSpace.Opens ↥Y) :
219	    dualUnitRingSwapInv f W' ≫ dualUnitRingSwap f W' = 𝟙 _ := by
220	  haveI := isIso_ε_restrictScalars_appIso f W'
221	  simp [dualUnitRingSwapInv, dualUnitRingSwap]
222	
223	open Opposite in
224	/-- `dualUnitRingSwap` is a section of `dualUnitRingSwapInv` (`inv ε ≫ ε = 𝟙`). -/
225	@[simp] lemma dualUnitRingSwap_comp_dualUnitRingSwapInv {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : Y ⟶ X)
226	    [IsOpenImmersion f] (W' : TopologicalSpace.Opens ↥Y) :
227	    dualUnitRingSwap f W' ≫ dualUnitRingSwapInv f W' = 𝟙 _ := by
228	  haveI := isIso_ε_restrictScalars_appIso f W'
229	  simp [dualUnitRingSwapInv, dualUnitRingSwap]
230	
231	open Opposite in
232	/-- **`invFun` codomain ε is an iso (`.hom` direction).**  The lax-monoidal unit `ε` of
233	`restrictScalars` along `(f.appIso W').hom` (the `.hom`, not `.inv`, of the structure ring iso) is
234	an isomorphism, since `(f.appIso W').hom` is a bijective ring map.  This powers the `invFun`
235	codomain swap (which reindexes the `Over V` section back across `f.opensFunctor` using the
236	`.hom` direction, the mirror of `dualUnitRingSwap`'s `.inv`). -/
237	lemma isIso_ε_restrictScalars_appIso_hom {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : Y ⟶ X) [IsOpenImmersion f]
238	    (W' : TopologicalSpace.Opens ↥Y) :
239	    IsIso (Functor.LaxMonoidal.ε
240	      (ModuleCat.restrictScalars (Scheme.Hom.appIso f W').hom.hom)) :=
241	  restrictScalars_isIso_ε_of_bijective (Scheme.Hom.appIso f W').hom.hom
242	    (CategoryTheory.ConcreteCategory.bijective_of_isIso (Scheme.Hom.appIso f W').hom)
243	
244	open Opposite in
245	/-- **`invFun` codomain unit ring-iso swap** `restrictScalars (f.appIso W').hom (𝟙_Y(W')) ⟶
246	𝟙_X(fW')`.  It is the inverse of the lax-monoidal unit `ε (restrictScalars (f.appIso W').hom)`,
247	an isomorphism by `isIso_ε_restrictScalars_appIso_hom`.  This is the codomain swap of the reverse
248	transport `invFun` (mirror of `dualUnitRingSwap`, using the `.hom` direction). -/
249	noncomputable def dualUnitRingSwapHom {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : Y ⟶ X) [IsOpenImmersion f]
250	    (W' : TopologicalSpace.Opens ↥Y) :
251	    (ModuleCat.restrictScalars (Scheme.Hom.appIso f W').hom.hom).obj
252	        (𝟙_ (ModuleCat ↑(Y.presheaf.obj (op W')))) ⟶
253	      𝟙_ (ModuleCat ↑(X.presheaf.obj (op ((Scheme.Hom.opensFunctor f).obj W')))) :=
254	  haveI := isIso_ε_restrictScalars_appIso_hom f W'
255	  CategoryTheory.inv (Functor.LaxMonoidal.ε
256	    (ModuleCat.restrictScalars (Scheme.Hom.appIso f W').hom.hom))
257	
258	open Opposite in
259	/-- **ε is an iso for the section-ring relabel** `X.presheaf.map (eqToHom e)` (an `eqToHom`-induced,
260	hence bijective, ring map between section rings `𝒪_X(b) → 𝒪_X(a)` for `a = b`).  Phrased at the
261	`X.presheaf` (`CommRingCat`) carrier so `CommRing` is native (`analogies/ma-legb262.md`). -/
262	lemma isIso_ε_restrictScalars_presheafMap {X : Scheme.{u}}
263	    {a b : (TopologicalSpace.Opens ↥X)ᵒᵖ} (e : a = b) :
264	    IsIso (Functor.LaxMonoidal.ε
265	      (ModuleCat.restrictScalars (X.presheaf.map (eqToHom e)).hom)) :=
266	  restrictScalars_isIso_ε_of_bijective (X.presheaf.map (eqToHom e)).hom
267	    (CategoryTheory.ConcreteCategory.bijective_of_isIso (X.presheaf.map (eqToHom e)))
268	
269	open Opposite in
270	/-- **Unit-section relabel swap** `restrictScalars (X.presheaf.map (eqToHom e)) (𝟙_X(b)) ⟶ 𝟙_X(a)`
271	for `a = b` (section opens of `X`).  It is `inv ε` of the relabel ring map, an isomorphism by
272	`isIso_ε_restrictScalars_presheafMap`.  This is the `?unit` codomain transport of
273	`sliceDualTransportInv`'s reverse component (mirror of `dualUnitRingSwap` for the `he`-relabel). -/
274	noncomputable def unitRelabelSwap {X : Scheme.{u}}
275	    {a b : (TopologicalSpace.Opens ↥X)ᵒᵖ} (e : a = b) :
276	    (ModuleCat.restrictScalars (X.presheaf.map (eqToHom e)).hom).obj
277	        (𝟙_ (ModuleCat ↑(X.presheaf.obj b))) ⟶
278	      𝟙_ (ModuleCat ↑(X.presheaf.obj a)) :=
279	  haveI := isIso_ε_restrictScalars_presheafMap e
280	  CategoryTheory.inv (Functor.LaxMonoidal.ε
281	    (ModuleCat.restrictScalars (X.presheaf.map (eqToHom e)).hom))
282	
283	open PresheafOfModules InternalHom Opposite in
284	/-- **Reverse slice transport (the `invFun` of `sliceDualTransport`), extracted top-level.**
285	
286	Given a dual section `ψ : restr V ((pushforward β).obj M.val) ⟶ restr V 𝟙_Y` over `Over V`,
287	this produces the X-slice dual section `restr fV M.val ⟶ restr fV 𝟙_X` over `Over fV`
288	(`fV = f.opensFunctor.obj V.unop`), the mirror of `sliceDualTransport`'s forward `toFun`.
289	
290	For `W'' : (Over fV)ᵒᵖ`, set `P := f⁻¹ᵁ W''.left` (so `f.opensFunctor.obj P = W''.left` only
291	propositionally, via `image_preimage_of_le` since `fV ⊆ range f`).  The component at `W''` is the
292	X-slice mirror of the forward component, conjugated by the `eqToHom`s from `image_preimage_of_le`
293	(mirror of `homLocalSection`):
294	`eqToHom … ≫ (restrictScalars (f.appIso P).hom.hom).map (ψ.app (op (Over.mk (homOfLE hPV)))) ≫
295	  dualUnitRingSwapHom f P`,
296	the codomain swap being `dualUnitRingSwapHom = inv (ε (restrictScalars (f.appIso P).hom.hom))`
297	(the `.hom`-direction `inv ε`). -/
298	noncomputable def sliceDualTransportInv {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : Y ⟶ X) [IsOpenImmersion f]
299	    (M : X.Modules) (V : (TopologicalSpace.Opens ↥Y)ᵒᵖ)
300	    (β : Y.ringCatSheaf.obj ⟶ (Hom.opensFunctor f).op ⋙ X.ringCatSheaf.obj)
301	    -- β-compatibility (iter-303): `β` is the open-immersion structure ring iso `(f.appIso).inv`,
302	    -- so post-composing it with `(f.appIso P).hom` is the identity on `𝒪_X(f''ᵁP)`.  This is the
303	    -- load-bearing ring identity that collapses the double `restrictScalars` in the reverse
304	    -- component (`?collapse`); it is FALSE for an arbitrary `β`, hence supplied as a hypothesis and
305	    -- discharged at the unique caller (`sliceDualTransport.invFun`) via `Iso.hom_inv_id`.
306	    (hβ : ∀ (P : TopologicalSpace.Opens ↥Y),
307	        ((β.app (op P)).hom).comp ((Scheme.Hom.appIso f P).hom.hom) = RingHom.id _)
308	    (ψ : (((PresheafOfModules.pushforward β).obj M.val).dual.obj V : Type u)) :
309	    (((PresheafOfModules.pushforward β).obj M.val.dual).obj V : Type u) := by
310	  refine { app := fun W'' => ?_, naturality := ?_ }
311	  · -- app component at `W''` (over `fV`).  `W' := (unop W'').left ≤ fV`; `P := f⁻¹ᵁ W'`.
312	    -- The down-set facts are established (axiom-clean); the morphism itself is the documented
313	    -- residual below.
314	    set W' := (unop W'').left with hW'
315	    have hW'fV : W' ≤ f ''ᵁ (unop V) := (unop W'').hom.le
316	    have hPV : f ⁻¹ᵁ W' ≤ unop V :=
317	      le_trans ((TopologicalSpace.Opens.map f.base).monotone hW'fV)
318	        (le_of_eq (f.preimage_image_eq (unop V)))
319	    have he : f ''ᵁ (f ⁻¹ᵁ W') = W' := by
320	      rw [Scheme.Hom.image_preimage_eq_opensRange_inf]
321	      exact inf_eq_right.mpr (hW'fV.trans (f.image_le_opensRange (unop V)))
322	    -- **app component — CLOSED axiom-clean (iter-303).**  The X-slice mirror of the forward
323	    -- `toFun`, conjugated across the propositional preimage round-trip `he : f''ᵁ(f⁻¹ᵁ W') = W'`.
324	    -- It is the four-leg composite (all legs concrete):
325	    --   (1) `M.val.map (eqToHom (op he.symm))` : source relabel `M.val(W') ⟶ restr_ρ M.val(fP)`
326	    --       (SEMILINEAR — codomain restricted along `ρ = X.ringCatSheaf.map (eqToHom (op he.symm))`,
327	    --       crossing the `𝒪_X(W') ↔ 𝒪_X(fP)` fiber);
328	    --   (2) `restrictScalars ρ |>.map (?collapse ≫ core)` transports the in-fiber-`fP` core:
329	    --       `?collapse` (the double-restrict collapse `M.val(fP) ≅ restrictScalars (f.appIso P).hom
330	    --       (restrictScalars (β.app P) (M.val fP))` via `restrictScalarsId'App` + `restrictScalarsComp'App`
331	    --       fed the ring identity `hβ (f⁻¹ᵁ W')`), and `core` (legs (3) ψ-reindex `restrictScalars
332	    --       (f.appIso P).hom |>.map (ψ.app …)` + (4) codomain unit swap `dualUnitRingSwapHom f P`);
333	    --   (3) `unitRelabelSwap (op he.symm)` : the codomain unit transport `restrictScalars ρ 𝟙_X(fP)
334	    --       ⟶ 𝟙_X(W')` (`inv ε` of the relabel, the new top-level helper).
335	    -- The cross-fiber transport (a single `≫`-chain cannot express it — the relabel is semilinear)
336	    -- is realised by applying the functor `restrictScalars ρ` to the in-fiber-`fP` core.
337	    -- **core (legs 3+4): VERIFIED well-formed in fiber `𝒪_X(fP)` (iter-303).**  The ψ-reindex
338	    -- `restrictScalars (f.appIso P).hom ∘ ψ.app` post-composed with the codomain unit swap
339	    -- `dualUnitRingSwapHom f P` assembles into
340	    --   `core : restrictScalars (f.appIso P).hom ((pushforward β M.val)(P)) ⟶ 𝟙_X(fP)`,
341	    -- a morphism of `ModuleCat 𝒪_X(fP)`.  (NB: the leg-3 target `restrictScalars (f.appIso P).hom
342	    -- ((restr V 𝟙_Y)-section)` DID defeq-unify with leg-4's `restrictScalars (f.appIso P).hom
343	    -- (𝟙_ (ModuleCat 𝒪_Y(P)))` — the unit-spelling reconciles here, exactly as in the closed
344	    -- forward `toFun`.)
345	    have core := (ModuleCat.restrictScalars (Scheme.Hom.appIso f (f ⁻¹ᵁ W')).hom.hom).map
346	        (ψ.app (op (Over.mk (homOfLE hPV)))) ≫ dualUnitRingSwapHom f (f ⁻¹ᵁ W')
347	    -- **Cross-fiber transport — CLOSED (iter-303).**  The goal lives in `ModuleCat 𝒪_X(W')` but
348	    -- `core` lives in `ModuleCat 𝒪_X(fP)` (`fP = f''ᵁf⁻¹ᵁW'`, propositionally `= W'` via `he`, but
349	    -- the section RINGS `𝒪_X(W')` / `𝒪_X(fP)` are only propositionally equal).  The source relabel
350	    -- `M.val(W') ⟶ M.val(fP)` is `M.val.map (eqToHom (op he.symm))` — SEMILINEAR, landing in
351	    -- `restrictScalars (X.ringCatSheaf.map (eqToHom …))`; combined with the source double-restrict
352	    -- collapse `restrictScalars (f.appIso P).hom ∘ restrictScalars (β.app P) ≅ restrictScalars 𝟙
353	    -- ≅ id` (ring identity `hβ (f⁻¹ᵁ W')`: `(β.app P).hom ∘ (f.appIso P).hom.hom = 𝟙_{𝒪_X(fP)}`,
354	    -- collapsed by `ModuleCat.restrictScalarsComp'App` + `restrictScalarsId'App`).  A single
355	    -- `≫`-chain in one `ModuleCat` cannot express this — the relabel crosses ring fibers — so `core`
356	    -- is conjugated across the `𝒪_X(fP) ↔ 𝒪_X(W')` fiber by applying the functor
357	    -- `restrictScalars (X.ringCatSheaf.map (eqToHom (op he.symm)))` to `?collapse ≫ core` (per memory
358	    -- `ts271-slicedualtransportinv`).  This cross-fiber transport is the next fine-grained target.
359	    refine M.val.map (eqToHom (congrArg op he.symm)) ≫
360	      (ModuleCat.restrictScalars ((X.ringCatSheaf.obj.map (eqToHom (congrArg op he.symm))).hom)).map
361	        (?collapse ≫ core) ≫ ?unit
362	    case collapse =>
363	      -- Collapse the double `restrictScalars` on `M.val(fP)` to the identity, using the ring
364	      -- identity `hβ (f⁻¹ᵁ W')` (`(β.app P).hom ∘ (f.appIso P).hom = 𝟙`).
365	      exact (ModuleCat.restrictScalarsId'App _ (hβ (f ⁻¹ᵁ W'))
366	            (M.val.obj (op (f ''ᵁ f ⁻¹ᵁ W')))).inv ≫
367	        (ModuleCat.restrictScalarsComp'App ((Scheme.Hom.appIso f (f ⁻¹ᵁ W')).hom.hom)
368	            ((β.app (op (f ⁻¹ᵁ W'))).hom) _ rfl (M.val.obj (op (f ''ᵁ f ⁻¹ᵁ W')))).hom

4510	  assembled via \(\mathtt{LinearEquiv.toModuleIso}\), with both module structures
4511	  identified explicitly.

4513	  \emph{Leg (A): the slice-Hom reindexing, built categorically.} A dual section
4514	  \(\varphi\) of the left-hand value is a morphism \(\mathtt{restr}_{fV}\,M.\mathtt{val} \to
4515	  \mathtt{restr}_{fV}\,\mathbf{1}_X\) over \(\mathtt{Over}\,(fV)\). Its image is the dual
4516	  section over \(\mathtt{Over}\,V\) whose component at \(W \le V\) (with image

4518	  \[

4520	      \bigl(\varphi_{(\mathtt{Over.mk}\,(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}(W \le V)))}\bigr),
4521	  \]

4523	  \(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\) by restriction of scalars along \(\beta_W\), and then

4527	  The codomain of the reindexed component is the restriction of scalars of the unit section,
4528	  which must be carried to the unit section over \(Y\). This is exactly the inverse of the
4529	  lax-monoidal unit comparison of the restriction-of-scalars functor,
4530	  \[
4531	    \mathtt{codomainMap} \;:=\;
4532	      \mathtt{inv}\bigl(\varepsilon(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,g)\bigr),
4533	      \qquad g := (f.\mathtt{appIso}\,W).\mathrm{inv}.\mathrm{hom},

4543	  \emph{Inverse.} The inverse reindexing depends on a set-theoretic down-set fact: because
4544	  \(f\) is an open immersion, \(fV \subseteq \mathrm{range}\,f\), so every open

4547	  \(\mathtt{IsOpenImmersion}\): an open contained in the (open) image of the immersion is the
4548	  functor-image of its own preimage. The project records it as the down-set identity
4549	  \(\iota_!(\iota^{-1}V) = V\) for \(V \le W\) (the helper
4550	  \(\mathtt{image\_preimage\_of\_le}\) (\cref{lem:image_preimage_of_le})),
4551	  and it is exactly the lemma the inverse reindexing relies on to define the components on the
4552	  whole down-set of \(fV\).

4554	  With this in hand the inverse \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Hom}\) mirrors the forward
4555	  component formula, with two changes: it uses \((f.\mathtt{appIso}\,W'').\mathrm{hom}\) in
4556	  place of its inverse, and its \(\varepsilon\)-codomain swap is
4557	  \(\mathtt{dualUnitRingSwapHom} = \mathtt{inv}\bigl(\varepsilon(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,
4558	  (f.\mathtt{appIso}\,W'').\mathrm{hom})\bigr)\) (\cref{def:dual_unit_ring_swap_hom})
4559	  --- that is, the \emph{inverse} of \(\varepsilon\) in the \(\mathrm{hom}\)-direction (where
4560	  \(\varepsilon\) is invertible by

4562	  \(\varepsilon\) of the \(\mathrm{inv}\)-direction \(\beta\). (The reverse reindex turns an
4563	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(fW'')\)-section map into an \(\mathcal{O}_Y(W'')\)-map, so it must restrict
4564	  along the \(\mathrm{hom}\)-direction ring iso, whose codomain swap is therefore \(\mathtt{inv}\,
4565	  \varepsilon\) of \emph{that} functor.) The whole family is reindexed by the inverse of the
4566	  down-set bijection \(W'' \leftrightarrow f^{-1}W''\) above. The round-trip identities \(\mathtt{left\_inv}\)
4567	  and \(\mathtt{right\_inv}\) then close component-wise by \(\mathtt{Iso.inv\_hom\_id}\) and
4568	  \(\mathtt{Iso.hom\_inv\_id}\) of \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\) (the forward/inverse \(\beta\)-swaps
4569	  cancel) together with the cancellation of the down-set bijection
4570	  \(\iota_!(\iota^{-1}V) = V\) against its inverse.
4571

4573	  module structure carries the left-hand value. The additive and scalar structure on a dual
4574	  section of the left-hand value

4576	  is the \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-module structure on the internal-hom object
4577	  (\cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value}) --- the pointwise module structure in which the
4578	  sum and scalar multiple of dual sections are formed at the terminal object \(\mathrm{id}_{fV}\)
4579	  of the slice over \(fV\). This agrees with, but is \emph{a priori} a distinct presentation of,
4580	  the additive and scalar structure of the underlying presheaf morphism (the pointwise add and
4581	  scalar action on morphisms of presheaves of modules). The verification of additivity and
4582	  \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linearity of the transport therefore proceeds in three distinct steps, the
4583	  first of which is a required first move rather than an afterthought:
4584	  \begin{enumerate}

4586	      addition and scalar action are identified with the underlying pointwise add and scalar action
4587	      on presheaf morphisms. This is a definitional identification of the two
4588	      \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-module presentations of the same group of dual sections: the
4589	      internal-hom operations, formed at the terminal object of the slice, are precisely the
4590	      pointwise morphism-level operations of the presheaf morphism they package. Until this
4591	      identification is made, the additivity and scalar-compatibility obligations of the transport
4592	      cannot be read off the change-of-rings compatibility, because their sums and scalar multiples
4593	      are taken in the \emph{internal-hom} structure, not the morphism structure.
4594	    \item[(ii)] \emph{Match the X-side and Y-side scalars by the \(\beta\)-naturality ring
4595	      identity.} The scalar acting on the X-side (the pushforward-restricted scalar \(s\) by
4596	      which \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\beta_W\) multiplies the reindexed component) and the
4597	      scalar acting on the Y-side (the \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-scalar \(c\) of the internal-hom
4598	      action) are \emph{a priori} elements of different rings and cannot be compared directly.
4599	      The bridge is the ring identity
4600	      \[
4601	        s \;=\; (\beta.\mathtt{app}\,W').\mathrm{hom}\, c,
4602	      \]
4603	      obtained from \(\mathtt{InternalHom.termRingMap\_naturality}\) --- which records how the
4604	      internal-hom term-ring map transports the scalar action across the slice --- together with
4605	      the naturality of \(\beta\) on the thin poset \(\mathrm{Opens}\,Y\). Without this identity
4606	      the two sides of the scalar-compatibility equation are not even of matching type, so it
4607	      must be supplied before the change-of-rings compatibility of step (iii) can be invoked.
4608	      The restricted scalar action is made explicit by
4609	      \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars.smul\_def'}\), which exhibits the restricted smul
4610	      as the original smul along \(\beta_W\).
4611	    \item[(iii)] \emph{Apply the change-of-rings compatibility on the morphism level.} Once the
4612	      operations are expressed pointwise on the underlying morphisms and the scalars matched by
4613	      (ii), additivity and
4614	      \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linearity follow from the post-composition compatibility of the
4615	      change-of-rings reindexing: the functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\beta_W\) preserves sums
4616	      and scalar multiples of morphisms, and the structure scalar is intertwined by the ring
4617	      isomorphism \(\beta_W\). This last is the presheaf-level shadow of
4618	      \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}.
4619	  \end{enumerate}
4620	
4621	  \emph{Naturality of the section family in \(W\).} This splits into two obligations of
4622	  different character, and it is a mistake to discharge the whole square by
4623	  \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\) alone. (a) The underlying \emph{base}-morphism naturality ---
4624	  that the two routes around a square agree on the \emph{indexing} morphisms of
4625	  \((\mathtt{Over}\,V.\mathrm{unop})^{\mathrm{op}}\) --- holds by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\),
4626	  since \(\mathrm{Opens}\,Y\) is a thin poset and there is at most one inclusion between any
4627	  two objects; this discharges \emph{only} the uniqueness of the base morphisms. (b) The
4628	  actual naturality obligation, however, is an equation between
4629	  \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-\emph{module} maps, and it is \emph{not} settled by (a). Once the base
4630	  morphisms are pinned by (a), the module-map equation reduces to the \(\varepsilon\)-naturality
4631	  square of \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) --- the naturality of the lax-monoidal unit
4632	  \(\varepsilon\) with respect to the restriction maps of \emph{both} the source and the target
4633	  presheaf, along the structure-ring isomorphism \(\beta_W\) --- post-composed with the

4635	  \varepsilon(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\beta_W)\). This \(\varepsilon\)-naturality step is a
4636	  genuine, separate obligation; the thin-poset argument of (a) does \emph{not} discharge it.
4637	  % NOTE: the Lean helper that delivers step (b) is

4639	  The required \(\varepsilon\)-commutativity-with-restriction-maps square is supplied by the

4642	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.}\varepsilon(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,
4643	  (\alpha.\mathrm{app}\,X).\mathrm{hom})\): its \(\mathtt{NatTrans}\) naturality field is
4644	  \emph{exactly} the square asserting that \(\varepsilon\) commutes with the restriction maps

4647	  of (a) settles only the base morphisms.

4927	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom,
4928	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom} (tag area 01CM).}
4929	  Let \(f : Y \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion of schemes, with image the open
4930	  \(U \subseteq X\), and let \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Then there is a
4931	  canonical isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-modules
4932	  \[
4933	    (\mathtt{dual}\,M)\big|_f \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathtt{dual}\bigl(M|_f\bigr),
4934	  \]
4935	  natural in \(M\), between the restriction of the sheaf-level dual
4936	  (\cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}) and the dual of the restriction. (Only opens
4937	  \(V \subseteq U\) intervene downstream, where the comparison is needed.)

4940	\begin{proof}
4941	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv, lem:slice_dual_transport}
4942	  This is the classical internal-hom base-change isomorphism
4943	  \(f^*\mathcal{H}\!om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(M, \mathcal{O}_X) \cong
4944	  \mathcal{H}\!om_{\mathcal{O}_Y}(f^*M, f^*\mathcal{O}_X)\) of
4945	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom}, specialised to the structure sheaf
4946	  \(\mathcal{G} = \mathcal{O}_X\). For an open immersion the morphism \(f\) is flat and
4947	  restriction \((-)|_f\) is the exact pullback \(f^*\), so the finite-presentation and
4948	  flatness hypotheses of the general ringed-space statement are automatic; this is the
4949	  \emph{favorable} (open-immersion) case of the base-change comparison, in which the map
4950	  is an isomorphism for \emph{every} \(M\), not only the finitely-presented ones.
4951	
4952	  We emphasise at the outset that this is a genuine \emph{natural isomorphism}, not a
4953	  definitional equality, and --- crucially --- that the dual is \emph{not sectionwise}.
4954	  Unlike the tensor product, whose value is computed fibrewise
4955	  (\((M \otimes N)(V) = M(V) \otimes N(V)\)), the dual evaluates to a Hom over an entire
4956	  down-set: for an open \(V \subseteq U\),
4957	  \[
4958	    (\mathtt{dual}\,M)(V)
4959	      \;=\; \mathcal{H}om\bigl(M|_V,\ \mathcal{O}|_V\bigr)\big|_{\mathtt{Over}\,V},
4960	  \]
4961	  a module of morphisms of presheaves over the whole slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,V \subseteq
4962	  \mathrm{Opens}\,Y\), \emph{not} a single fibre \(M(V) \to \mathcal{O}(V)\). Consequently
4963	  the assertion that restriction along the open immersion \(f\) commutes with the dual does
4964	  \emph{not} reduce to a single ground-ring reconciliation. The proof proceeds in two
4965	  stages: a preliminary adjunction-uniqueness rewrite (stage~H1) that re-presents the
4966	  pulled-back dual in pushforward form, followed by the resolution of the resulting
4967	  \emph{residual} into \emph{two} legs, carried out objectwise in the open \(V\) (with
4968	  \(V \subseteq U\)) and assembled over the slice with poset-thin naturality.
4969	
4970	  \emph{Stage H1 --- adjunction-uniqueness rewrite.} The pulled-back dual is initially
4971	  presented in the \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\)-form inherited from the abstract
4972	  definition of the restriction functor, and no sectionwise comparison can begin against
4973	  that opaque form. We first rewrite it into a \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\)-form: the
4974	  restriction-along-an-open-immersion pullback and the pushforward along the structure-ring
4975	  map \(\beta\) are both left adjoints to one and the same functor, so by uniqueness of left
4976	  adjoints --- the adjunction \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\) composed with
4977	  \(\mathtt{leftAdjointUniq}\) --- they are canonically isomorphic, and this isomorphism
4978	  transports the pulled-back dual into pushforward form. After H1 the residual obligation is
4979	  \emph{exactly}
4980	  \[
4981	    (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathtt{obj}\,(\mathtt{dual}\,M.\mathtt{val})
4982	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
4983	    \mathtt{dual}\bigl((\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathtt{obj}\,M.\mathtt{val}\bigr),
4984	  \]
4985	  i.e.\ ``\(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\) commutes with the dual''. It is this residual ---
4986	  \emph{not} the original Step-4 isomorphism --- that legs (A) and (B) below resolve; legs
4987	  (A) and (B) are the content remaining \emph{after} the H1 rewrite, not a standalone
4988	  two-leg split of the whole step.
4989

4905	
4906	\begin{lemma}
4907	
4908	\leanok
4909	[Restriction commutes with the dual along an open immersion (the C-bridge)]
4910	  \label{lem:dual_restrict_iso}
4911	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dual_restrict_iso}
4912	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv, lem:slice_dual_transport}
4913	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom,
4914	  % Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom} (tag area 01CM)
4915	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3710--L3721).
4916	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
4917	  % "Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ be a morphism
4918	  % of ringed spaces. Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_Y$-modules.
4919	  % If $\mathcal{F}$ is finitely presented and $f$ is flat,
4920	  % then the canonical map
4921	  % $$
4922	  % f^*\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_Y}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})
4923	  % \longrightarrow
4924	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(f^*\mathcal{F}, f^*\mathcal{G})
4925	  % $$
4926	  % is an isomorphism."
4927	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom,
4928	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom} (tag area 01CM).}
4929	  Let \(f : Y \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion of schemes, with image the open
4930	  \(U \subseteq X\), and let \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Then there is a
4931	  canonical isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-modules
4932	  \[
4933	    (\mathtt{dual}\,M)\big|_f \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathtt{dual}\bigl(M|_f\bigr),
4934	  \]
4935	  natural in \(M\), between the restriction of the sheaf-level dual
4936	  (\cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}) and the dual of the restriction. (Only opens
4937	  \(V \subseteq U\) intervene downstream, where the comparison is needed.)
4938	\end{lemma}
4939	
4940	\begin{proof}
4941	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv, lem:slice_dual_transport}
4942	  This is the classical internal-hom base-change isomorphism
4943	  \(f^*\mathcal{H}\!om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(M, \mathcal{O}_X) \cong
4944	  \mathcal{H}\!om_{\mathcal{O}_Y}(f^*M, f^*\mathcal{O}_X)\) of
4945	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom}, specialised to the structure sheaf
4946	  \(\mathcal{G} = \mathcal{O}_X\). For an open immersion the morphism \(f\) is flat and
4947	  restriction \((-)|_f\) is the exact pullback \(f^*\), so the finite-presentation and
4948	  flatness hypotheses of the general ringed-space statement are automatic; this is the
4949	  \emph{favorable} (open-immersion) case of the base-change comparison, in which the map
4950	  is an isomorphism for \emph{every} \(M\), not only the finitely-presented ones.
4951	
4952	  We emphasise at the outset that this is a genuine \emph{natural isomorphism}, not a
4953	  definitional equality, and --- crucially --- that the dual is \emph{not sectionwise}.
4954	  Unlike the tensor product, whose value is computed fibrewise
4955	  (\((M \otimes N)(V) = M(V) \otimes N(V)\)), the dual evaluates to a Hom over an entire
4956	  down-set: for an open \(V \subseteq U\),
4957	  \[
4958	    (\mathtt{dual}\,M)(V)
4959	      \;=\; \mathcal{H}om\bigl(M|_V,\ \mathcal{O}|_V\bigr)\big|_{\mathtt{Over}\,V},
4960	  \]
4961	  a module of morphisms of presheaves over the whole slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,V \subseteq
4962	  \mathrm{Opens}\,Y\), \emph{not} a single fibre \(M(V) \to \mathcal{O}(V)\). Consequently
4963	  the assertion that restriction along the open immersion \(f\) commutes with the dual does
4964	  \emph{not} reduce to a single ground-ring reconciliation. The proof proceeds in two
4965	  stages: a preliminary adjunction-uniqueness rewrite (stage~H1) that re-presents the
4966	  pulled-back dual in pushforward form, followed by the resolution of the resulting
4967	  \emph{residual} into \emph{two} legs, carried out objectwise in the open \(V\) (with
4968	  \(V \subseteq U\)) and assembled over the slice with poset-thin naturality.
4969	
4970	  \emph{Stage H1 --- adjunction-uniqueness rewrite.} The pulled-back dual is initially
4971	  presented in the \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\)-form inherited from the abstract
4972	  definition of the restriction functor, and no sectionwise comparison can begin against
4973	  that opaque form. We first rewrite it into a \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\)-form: the
4974	  restriction-along-an-open-immersion pullback and the pushforward along the structure-ring
4975	  map \(\beta\) are both left adjoints to one and the same functor, so by uniqueness of left
4976	  adjoints --- the adjunction \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\) composed with
4977	  \(\mathtt{leftAdjointUniq}\) --- they are canonically isomorphic, and this isomorphism
4978	  transports the pulled-back dual into pushforward form. After H1 the residual obligation is
4979	  \emph{exactly}
4980	  \[
4981	    (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathtt{obj}\,(\mathtt{dual}\,M.\mathtt{val})
4982	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
4983	    \mathtt{dual}\bigl((\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathtt{obj}\,M.\mathtt{val}\bigr),
4984	  \]
4985	  i.e.\ ``\(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\) commutes with the dual''. It is this residual ---
4986	  \emph{not} the original Step-4 isomorphism --- that legs (A) and (B) below resolve; legs
4987	  (A) and (B) are the content remaining \emph{after} the H1 rewrite, not a standalone
4988	  two-leg split of the whole step.
4989	
4990	  \emph{Leg (A) --- slice-site Hom base-change (Beck--Chevalley).} The domain presheaf must
4991	  first be transported across the open immersion. Because \(f\) is an open immersion, its
4992	  direct-image functor on open sets is fully faithful with image
4993	  the down-set \(\{W : W \le U\}\); this carries the restricted domain
4994	  \(\mathtt{restr}_V(f_*M)\) over \(\mathtt{Over}\,V \subseteq \mathrm{Opens}\,Y\) to the
4995	  restricted domain \(\mathtt{restr}_{fV}\,M\) over \(\mathtt{Over}\,(fV) \subseteq
4996	  \mathrm{Opens}\,X\), and hence identifies the two Hom-modules computed over these matched
4997	  slices. This is a genuine slice-site Hom base-change --- a Beck--Chevalley square on the
4998	  internal hom across the fully-faithful open-immersion functor, restricted to the down-set
4999	  of \(fV\) --- \emph{not} a fibrewise identity.
5000	
5001	  \emph{Leg (B) --- ground-ring reconciliation.} The codomain of the internal hom is in
5002	  both cases the structure sheaf. Once leg (A) has matched the domains, the unit codomain
5003	  is reconciled along the open-immersion ring isomorphism
5004	  \(\beta_V = (\mathtt{toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}_V \colon
5005	  \mathcal{O}_X(fV) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_Y(V)\): restriction of scalars along the
5006	  ring \emph{isomorphism} \(\beta_V\) is an equivalence, and its ModuleCat-level
5007	  shadow on duals is exactly \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}, carrying the
5008	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(fV)\)-linear dual onto the \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear dual,
5009	  \(R(V)\)-linearly and invertibly. This is the dual analogue, at the module level, of the
5010	  ring-isomorphism tensor equivalence (\texttt{restrictscalars\_ringiso\_strongmonoidal}).
5011	
5012	  \emph{The combined leg-(A)\(\circ\)(B) atom \(\mathtt{sliceDualTransport}\).} Both legs are
5013	  realised together by a single \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear isomorphism
5014	  \(\mathtt{sliceDualTransport}\,f\,M\,V\) (\cref{lem:slice_dual_transport}), built
5015	  \emph{by hand} --- it is \emph{not} obtained by consuming a sheaf-level slice equivalence.
5016	  This is essential: the equivalence of \emph{sheaves of modules} over an open immersion
5017	  compares the restriction functors and the units, but carries \emph{no} internal-hom or
5018	  dual content; the assertion realized by leg (A) --- that the dual commutes with slice
5019	  reindexing along the open-immersion direct-image functor \(f_{!}\) on opens
5020	  (\(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\)) --- cannot be supplied by such an object without a
5021	  monoidal-closed structure on sheaves of modules, which this development deliberately does
5022	  \emph{not} carry (cf.\ \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}). The atom must
5023	  therefore be constructed directly.
5024	
5025	  Concretely (\cref{lem:slice_dual_transport}), the forward component of
5026	  \(\mathtt{sliceDualTransport}\,f\,M\,V\) at \(W \le V\) is the categorical image
5027	  \((\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\beta_W).\mathrm{map}\,(\varphi_{\bullet})\) of the dual-section
5028	  component of \(\varphi\) at the \(f\)-image of \(W\), reindexed across
5029	  \(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\) by restriction of scalars along \(\beta_W\), post-composed with
5030	  the leg-(B) codomain swap \(\mathtt{inv}(\varepsilon(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\beta_W))\).
5031	  Leg (A) is built categorically via \((\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\beta_W).\mathrm{map}\):
5032	  a genuine change-of-rings functor acts here, so the reindexing must be applied as a
5033	  functor map rather than pointwise. Leg (B)'s unit comparison \(\varepsilon\) is invertible
5034	  because \(\beta_W\) is a ring isomorphism, as detailed in \cref{lem:slice_dual_transport}.
5035	  Because \(\mathrm{Opens}\,Y\) is a thin poset, the
5036	  naturality squares commute by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\). The forward and inverse maps
5037	  identify the slice-Hom value over \(\mathtt{Over}\,(fV)\) with the slice-Hom value over
5038	  \(\mathtt{Over}\,V\), \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linearly.
5039	
5040	  The Step-4 residual is then \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.isoMk}\) applied \emph{directly} to
5041	  the family \(V \mapsto \mathtt{sliceDualTransport}\,f\,M\,V\) --- which already packages
5042	  \emph{both} legs (A) and (B) --- with the outer naturality square thin-poset-trivial. No
5043	  separate leg-(B) step is interposed in \(\mathtt{isoMk}\): the per-section atom of
5044	  \cref{lem:slice_dual_transport} carries the full leg-(A)\(\circ\)(B) content. The outer
5045	  \(\mathtt{isoMk}\) naturality square follows from the per-section family naturality
5046	  \(\mathtt{sliceDualTransport.naturality}\) of \cref{lem:slice_dual_transport}: the
5047	  \(\varepsilon\)-naturality is established first at the level of individual sections, and the
5048	  outer presheaf-morphism naturality of \(\mathtt{isoMk}\) then follows.
5049	
5050	  The residual of stage~H1 (``\(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\) commutes with the dual'') is
5051	  the sectionwise composite of leg (A) followed by leg (B), packaged over the slice with
5052	  poset-thin naturality; the full Step-4 isomorphism is then the H1 isomorphism followed by
5053	  this composite. The slice-reindexing coherence is \emph{light}, because \(\mathrm{Opens}\)
5054	  is a thin poset and the relevant squares commute by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\),
5055	  whereas H1 and legs (A) and (B) carry the substantive content.
5056	
5057	  It is worth recording \emph{why} the strong-monoidal restriction
5058	  (\texttt{presheaf\_pushforward\_adj\_substrate}) has no direct dual analogue here. For
5059	  the tensor product the transport \emph{collapsed to leg (B) alone}: because \(\otimes\)
5060	  is sectionwise, pushing a tensor forward reduced, section by section, to a single
5061	  ground-ring tensor equivalence, with no domain slice left to re-index. The dual's
5062	  non-sectionwise nature removes that collapse and forces the additional leg (A): the
5063	  domain \(\mathtt{restr}_V(f_*M)\) genuinely lives over a varying slice and must be
5064	  transported before the ground ring can be reconciled.
5065	
5066	  We caution that the naive ``fixed-value-category slice equivalence'' of
5067	  \texttt{open\_immersion\_slice\_sheaf\_equiv} is \emph{not} applicable to leg (A). That instrument
5068	  operates in a fixed value category over a fixed base ring, whereas the residual of
5069	  leg (A) lives at the \emph{presheaf} level, with a per-section ring \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)
5070	  that varies with \(V\) and a finer slicing than the sheaf-level equivalence resolves;
5071	  leg (A) must therefore be built directly at the presheaf-of-modules / varying-ring level.
5072	
5073	  Composing legs (A) and (B) gives, for each \(V \subseteq U\), an
5074	  \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear isomorphism between the two values; assembling these into a
5075	  presheaf-of-modules natural isomorphism and sheafifying through the sheaf-level dual of
5076	  \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} resolves the H1 residual. Precomposing with the stage-H1
5077	  rewrite then yields the stated isomorphism
5078	  \((\mathtt{dual}\,M)|_f \cong \mathtt{dual}(M|_f)\).
5079	
5080	  \emph{The inverse-uniqueness shortcut is not viable.} One might hope to derive the
5081	  isomorphism from the already-established \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} by uniqueness of
5082	  monoidal inverses --- the dual being the \(\otimes\)-inverse of an invertible module and
5083	  restriction commuting with \(\otimes\) --- sidestepping leg (A). The abstract statements of
5084	  this idiom (\(\mathtt{hasRightDualOfEquivalence}\), \(\mathtt{rightDualIso}\)) require,
5085	  however, four structures the present setting lacks: a registered \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}\)
5086	  instance on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) (the monoidal structure here is carried entirely
5087	  by hand), a \emph{strong} (not merely lax/oplax) monoidal restriction functor, restriction
5088	  being an \emph{equivalence} (false for a non-surjective open immersion, which is a
5089	  localization), and an exact pairing \(\mathtt{ExactPairing}\,M\,(\mathtt{dual}\,M)\) (which
5090	  exists only for dualizable \(M\), whereas the lemma is stated for general \(M\)). Even
5091	  restricted to the invertible consumer this would require building a coevaluation and
5092	  verifying the zig-zag identities --- strictly \emph{more} infrastructure than leg (A) --- so
5093	  the construction commits to legs (A) and (B) above.
5094	\end{proof}

5100	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_eval, lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
5101	  Let \(Y\) be a scheme. The sheaf-level dual of the structure sheaf is canonically the
5102	  structure sheaf itself,
5103	  \[
5104	    \mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_Y \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{O}_Y,
5105	  \]
5106	  obtained by sheafifying the presheaf-level ``evaluation at \(1\)'' isomorphism
5107	  \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathbf{1}, \mathbf{1}) \cong \mathbf{1}\): every endomorphism of the
5108	  unit presheaf is multiplication by its value at the global section \(1\), so evaluation
5109	  at \(1\) identifies the internal hom of the unit into itself with the unit.
5110	\end{lemma}
5111	
5112	\begin{proof}
5113	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_eval, lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, def:presheaf_dual_unit_iso}
5114	  At the presheaf level the dual of the unit \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_Y\) is the internal
5115	  hom \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathbf{1}, \mathbf{1})\), whose value on an open \(U\) is the module
5116	  of \(\mathcal{O}(U)\)-linear endomorphisms of \(\mathbf{1}|_U\). Each such endomorphism is
5117	  determined by its value at the global section \(1\) and equals multiplication by that
5118	  value; hence \emph{evaluation at \(1\)} is itself the sectionwise isomorphism
5119	  \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathbf{1}, \mathbf{1}) \cong \mathbf{1}\) of presheaves of modules, with
5120	  inverse the scalar-action map \(r \mapsto (\,\cdot\,r)\). (No left unitor is needed: the
5121	  identification is the direct evaluation-at-\(1\) / global-scalar-multiplication
5122	  isomorphism, not the composite through \(\mathbf{1} \otimes \mathbf{1} \cong \mathbf{1}\).)
5123	  Sheafifying through the sheaf-level dual of \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} yields
5124	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_Y \cong \mathcal{O}_Y\). The scheme-level alias of this
5125	  presheaf evaluation-at-\(1\) isomorphism is
5126	  \(\mathtt{presheafDualUnitIso}\) (\cref{def:presheaf_dual_unit_iso}).
5127	\end{proof}
